solution.answer: $H_k(\mathbb{C}P^n;\mathbb{Z}) \cong \mathbb{Z}$ for $k = 0, 2, 4, \ldots, 2n$, and $H_k = 0$ for all odd $k$ and for $k > 2n$.
steps: $\mathbb{C}P^n$ is built by $\mathbb{C}P^k = \mathbb{C}P^{k-1} \cup e^{2k}$, giving a CW structure with cells only in even dimensions $0, 2, 4, \ldots, 2n$.
The cellular chain complex has $C_{2k} = \mathbb{Z}$ for $0 \leq k \leq n$ and $C_{\text{odd}} = 0$.
Every boundary map $d_k: C_k \to C_{k-1}$ has $C_{k-1} = 0$ (since no odd cells) or $C_k = 0$, forcing $d_k = 0$.
Hence every chain is a cycle and every cycle is not a boundary: $H_{2k} = \mathbb{Z}$ for $0 \leq k \leq n$.
This is much simpler than $\mathbb{R}P^n$: no boundary map ever contributes torsion.
